speaker: interviewer
text: --Rosenroth. Interview with Daisy Miller, Beverly Hills. On camera, Peter [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? Marcia, ?] you were telling us about the party, the New Years Eve--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That was New Year's Eve. And at that time I lived in Dachau. I met a friend, and they gave us shelter in Dachau. And I start-- I always liked to do something. So I was working all of that day in Dachau, in the Jewish community. Because it was close to Munich. And a lot of people came. A lot of survivors came, and we had to register them, and to provide food. So this-- all that I did, start to-- start to make like a DP camp for the survivors. Anyway, living--

speaker: interviewer
text: This in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in Dachau-- in the city of Dachau-- because there where I met my friend and my friend's brother. And he had already a place where to live, so we went where we could live. Anyway, being what-- in New Year's Eve, I had a feeling that I'm going to-- my brother's still alive. He was healthy, he was handsome, and he was young. So I never-- he knew that we were alive, but I didn't know that he was alive. So I walked-- I took a train and went to Regensburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you think it would be Regensburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regensburg was the border of Germany and Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you thought that people were coming there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a border that, New Year's Eve, their patrol-- they were a Russian patrol-- they were probably drinking. It would be easier to cross the border, because we were constantly crossing borders. I forgot-- I didn't tell you the story what happened from Lodz. Can I go back, or finish this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I go back. When I went to Lodz to register and look for my-- for survivors, I met a whole group of people that I knew from-- from the ghetto. And I told them that I'm not going to-- we went to Lodz. And everywhere there were Russian [NON-ENGLISH]. And I had such a terrible feeling that I'm still in concentration camp. I was constantly watched. But they were good people. There were already the-- the Russian Army, who occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my friends, look. Now it's time to go. If you want to go, I work on the border of Czech and Germany. And if you want to go, I know a lot of people. The Russian-- the Russian war prisoners were working with me in camp. And they took over patrol over the border. So about 20 of my friends said, we're going with you. So I took them over the border to-- to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the reason I stayed in Dachau, because I had a friend there. I had the feeling-- that was New Year's Eve, and you're young. You don't think. You're jolly. So the Russian people, they were drinking. And I was hoping maybe my brother is coming. I didn't know. And my sister stayed in Poland with my friend, Marla, and her sister, Bella, which we lived together in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and took a train, and went to Regensburg. Being in Regensburg, they already had formed a DP camp, and it was New Year's Eve, so what do young people say? Let's go dancing. So go dancing. I didn't have anything to wear. I was still wearing my striped clothes and my wooden shoes. So one of my friends said, you know what? Wait a minute. 10 minutes later, he comes back with a big suitcase. And he said, here you are. I opened the suitcase. A lot of beautiful clothes. So I said, where did you get this from? He said, I stole it from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I got [GERMAN], and I went to the dance. All of a sudden I was dancing. This was-- we were liberated in May, and this was already January the January the 1st. And all of a sudden, I see young people are walking in with the Russian hats and the Russian furs. And a lot of young people are walking in. There was dancing. And I said to my partner who I was dancing with, I said, I think that's my brother. He said, you're crazy. I said, no. I left him in the middle of this floor, and I went over to the young man. I didn't see anybody. I saw him. And I said, Leo, that's you. So-- [SOB] I don't ever remember-- my sister stood next to him. And she said-- [SOB] she said, that's your sister. He did no recognize me. So I said, yes, that's me. The last time he saw me I was a little girl, 14 years old, and now I am 20. I changed a lot since. Anyway, I left everybody, stood, and I just could not believe how we got united at that dance. Now, I want to take my brother [SOB] into Germany to a DP camp. And he said, I'm not going. I'm afraid. Being in Russia for so many years, he was so brainwashed, he was-- prison in, prison out. It was-- conditions were so terrible, too. But somehow they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to Dachau, and I got them papers. I said to my friend, look, my brother survived and he is terribly brainwashed. You give him any paper-- I worked already at the Jewish committee that he survived. So they gave him a paper, and he went to a DP camp in Germany. [SOB] And ever since, we were united. Let's see, where did I stop before? At liberation. Yes, I'm sorry. I was so involved with the story about you meeting your brother again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was amazed

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were telling us about his coming and meeting you again. Your other-- your sister though, meantime, had stayed behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister stayed behind in Poland. And she said that she's going to wait for my brother. We didn't know that he was alive. We just were hoping that he's alive. He was healthy. He was young. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get word to her that you had found each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. This was going-- this was so fast. It was within probably a month. I said, I'm going back to the Czechoslovakia, and I'm going to register to-- I didn't want to stay under the Russian occupation. So I said, I'm going to register to go to the United States, because my brother wanted to go to Israel, and I wanted to go to the United States. I don't remember how-- oh, yeah. I met my husband in Germany. This was 1946, I met my husband. I had a cousin, Masza, living in Frankfurt. And I went. I found out that she's alive. We survived the war together. I helped her hiding, and I helped her with the little food I had. And she survived. And I found out. We all registered wherever we could to find the relatives and to find friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- I went to Frankfurt, and I met there my husband. And-- [SIGHING] this was 1945-- the end of 1945. I-- I'm sorry, I'm going back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war, I went to work for the American Joint. I didn't know what to do, so I thought I'm going to work. So I worked at the American Joint in Schleissheim. This is a city right in Germany, right near Salzburg. And we tried to there to get Jewish children. A lot of Jewish children were hidden in convents, and with-- with gentile families, so we tried to get them out. This was my job. But afterwards-- and we were very successful. We found a lot of Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- excuse me. Tell me again, where this-- because you mentioned Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Salzburg. This was a city in Austria. It was right near Bad Gastein. And it was a big-- it was-- it was, I was working there. Oh yeah. I hooked up with the Israelian brigade. I always liked to do something. And our job was to find out Jewish hidden children. There were a lot of Jewish hidden children in convents in France, in Germany-- not in Poland. Nobody would survive in Poland. And we tried to get the children out, and get them into Israel. So this is where I was working for about a short time. And then I went to work for the American Joint in Schleissheim, near Dachau. And I worked there for about maybe eight, nine months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I met my husband, but in Frankfurt. I was-- we were just traveling from city to city, from-- just-- we didn't have anything. We didn't need anything. We were young. We didn't know what we wanted to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a survivor. He survived he survived Aushwitz II. He was in concentration camp for many years. Maybe for four years he was. Anyway, I went to Germany. I quit my job. And I went to Germany. And I lived in Schleissheim by Frankfurt. And we got married in Frankfurt. And my sister and my brother, we lived together for about two, three years in Germany. And they wanted to go to Israel. So they signed up. And they went to Israel. And I wanted to go to the United States. So I registered with my husband. And I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1949, the end of 1949. For about five years, we lived in DP camps. We lived in Frankfurt. We lived in Munich. We lived in different DP camps. And I always worked at some kind of a job, either for the Joint or the Brigade, for different jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband? What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, he was a businessman. He was a very successful businessmen. He was very bright. He was born in [? Kalush, ?] and he was raised in a yeshiva. He never went to a public school. And we got married, 1946, in Frankfurt am Main. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to this country, where did you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to-- we came to this country in 19-- the end of 1949, we came to Buffalo, New York. We were sponsored to the American Joint. [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Buffalo for about-- my daughter was born there. My two children were born there. We lived in Buffalo for about maybe 12 years. And then life was difficult in Buffalo. And my husband was very bright. He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was always doing business. We had a liquor store. He was always doing some kind of business. So we came to Los Angeles in 19-- [INAUDIBLE] that was in 196-- we here about 30 years. So about '62, 1962. My husband bought a liquor store in Los Angeles. He was very bright. Right away, he was always doing some kind of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what then? You had two daughters? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My both daughters-- one of my daughters-- I was very much-- I was married five years in Germany, but I was very much afraid to have children. I had such a fear that the German doctors may injure my children. So I didn't-- I did a foolish thing. When you're young, you do stupid things. I aborted my first child, because I had that fear that they may do something to the child. But luckily, I got pregnant and my older daughter, Sharon, was born in the United States. She was born in Buffalo, New York. And it was hard, till we got on our feet. And after we lived in Buffalo for about 12 years, my husband said-- he was very bright-- he said that that's not life for us. He worked. People helped us-- American. He worked in a steel plant. And he said, that's not the life for me. We have to look to have a better life. So we came to Los Angeles, and he bought a liquor store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm a designer. I worked everywhere. I have my own studio. Clothes design, yes. And I was very, very successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away, when you came to Buffalo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. I always worked. Right away, because there was no money. We came with nothing. We came from concentration camp. And I worked here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always doing the same kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always doing the same thing. I'm a designer, and I'm a very good designer, I just want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, what kind of message would you want to leave for the world to remember, for your grandchildren? What would you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message I would like to say, that we all can live in peace. There is enough room in this world for everybody to live in peace. This was a terrible, terrible time, and this was a time-- only they were-- we were exterminated only because we were Jews. And Jews are actually very nice people. They're merciful people. They help everybody. They help the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for your grandchildren, the message is?

speaker: interviewee
text: You live in peace. You make peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very much, [? Marcia. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my husband's grandma. She was probably born in [? Kalush ?] in the 1800.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any pictures of yourself from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the only picture that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everything was taken away from us. We left Auschwitz from the bath house naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1946. I was working for the American Joint in Schleissheim, near Dachau. This picture was taken approximately two years after the war. That's my sister, Guta. She is two years younger than I am. And she lives in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guta Zawodnik. This is my cousin, Masza. This is when I found my cousin in a camp in Zeilsheim, near Frankfurt. We were reunited for the first time after six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her full name? [? Marcia ?] Grzesz. This is my husband, Jacob Rosenroth. He passed away three years ago. And we had a happy, happy life for 47 years. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken approximately 1950. This picture was taken in 1946, when I met my husband Jacob. I met him in a DP camp in Salzburg, near Frankfurt. This is my wedding picture. I was about 21 years old. I was wearing-- wearing a dress. Maybe 100 girls were wearing the same dress. For the tall one, it was short. For the short one, it was tall. This is with my husband. And all the flowers we had, as you see, they're branches of Christmas trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- by Frankfurt, in Zeilsheim. This is a picture of my wedding, with a little girl who survived the war. I don't remember where. And my cousin Maszra, which we always were together. She lives now in Chicago. This is a picture of my husband. We lived in Zeilsheim, in a DP camp, and my husband was very bright, and always was leading something. This picture was taken in 1948. My brother, he is a very loyal Zionist. And he went to Israel. And he was fighting the war of liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Leo Cichanowski. Now it's changed to Chain.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken in the United States. No. It was taken in-- it was taken in the United States when we came to America in 1949, the end of 1949. And we had a big gathering from all the newcomers. And somehow they picked me to show how European people look. What can I say? [LAUGHING] With my husband, Jacob. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Buffalo, New York, approximately in 1951, with my firstborn. These were my best day of my life. My little girl, Sharon. The first one on the left is my beautiful daughter Michelle. Next to her is her little girl, Amy. She's big now. Next to her is her lovely husband, Jerry, and their little baby, Molly. She's big now. Sitting in the center is my husband, Jacob. In the middle is my beautiful daughter, Sharon. Next to Sharon is her son-- which he was bar mitzvah-- Matthew. Next to Matthew is my beautiful-- my handsome son-in-law Brian. Sitting is me, [? Marcia. ?] And the little girl is my daughter's little girl, Rachel. I would like to introduce my daughter. I would like to introduce my daughter, Sharon Hersch. She always was a great joy to me. She is wonderful. She has a wonderful husband-- Brian-- and two beautiful children-- Matthew Hersch and Rachel Hersch. I could not ask for a better daughter. I love her dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my mom to volunteer to give this interview. As difficult as it was to listen to it, it must have been very painful and difficult for her to recall all the terrible memories that she had to go through in that period of her life. But I'm proud of her, that we will have the family legacy on tape, and it will be passed on to our children. And also, I want to let you know that I'm Sharon Rosenroth Hersch. And unfortunately, I'm going to be the last of the Rosenroths, and the last of the family to survive. Yeah. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say one more word? I have another daughter, which is Michelle Rosenroth Mendelsohn. She's working now. She's a teacher. She's a wonderful person, and very, very much involved in Jewish affairs. So is Sharon.
